title: Pork schnitzel
subtitle: Pork loin pounded thin, breaded, fried in butter and oil until crisp, served with lemon.
excerpt: The everyday cousin of Wiener Schnitzel. Pork loin slices are pounded thin, dredged in flour, egg, and breadcrumbs, then shallow-fried until the coating puffs up and lifts away from the meat. Served with a wedge of lemon and a parsley-and-butter spoon.
sourceNotes: Schnitzel as a category covers the central European tradition of pounded breaded cutlets. Wiener Schnitzel proper (Austrian, name-protected since 2007) is veal; pork schnitzel (Schweineschnitzel or Schnitzel Wiener Art, 'Vienna style') is the everyday version eaten throughout Germany, Austria, the Czech Republic, Hungary, and Poland. The technique appears in central European cookery books from the mid-nineteenth century. The looser, blistered coating that lifts away from the meat is a sign of correct frying: enough fat in the pan that the breadcrumb has somewhere to puff into.

recipe.freezeNotes: Breaded raw schnitzels freeze well between sheets of baking paper for up to two months. Fry from frozen, adding two minutes to each side.
recipe.batchNotes: Bread all the cutlets in one go, then fry in two pans simultaneously to keep up with the table. A schnitzel that has waited fifteen minutes for its turn at the pan is a different dish.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Breaded cutlets can rest on a rack in the fridge for up to two hours before frying; let them come back to room temperature for fifteen minutes before the pan.

content.text: Four pork loin steaks pounded to about 5 mm thick, breaded in flour-egg-breadcrumb, fried for two minutes a side in a pan with a generous depth of fat. The coating should puff and blister away from the meat, not sit tight against it. The trick is fat depth: half oil and half butter, roughly five millimetres deep. Less than that and the breadcrumb just toasts on the side facing the pan; more than that and it goes greasy.

A pork loin steak is what you want, not pork tenderloin (the lean strip), and not pork chops on the bone. Loin medallions trimmed of fat and pounded thin between cling film are tender and even. Salt the pork on both sides before breading; the salt seasons the meat before the egg coating seals it.

items.prepNote: 4 boneless loin steaks, around 150 g each

content.text: Trim any silver skin and external fat from the {{pork-loin}} of pork loin. Sandwich each steak between two sheets of cling film and pound with the smooth side of a meat mallet from the centre outwards until evenly 5 mm thick. The cutlet roughly doubles in surface area. Season generously with salt and pepper on both sides.

Set three wide bowls or plates in a line. Put the {{plain-flour}} of flour in the first, the {{eggs}} beaten egg in the second, the {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs in the third. Dip each cutlet into the flour and pat off the excess. Then into the egg, letting the surplus drip away. Then into the breadcrumbs; press lightly so the crumbs adhere without compacting. Lay on a rack.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil and half the {{unsalted-butter}} in a 30 cm frying pan over medium-high heat. The fat should be about 5 mm deep. When the butter has stopped foaming and a pinch of breadcrumb dropped in sizzles vigorously, lower in the first cutlet (or two, if the pan takes them without crowding). Swirl the pan once so the fat washes over the top of the cutlet. Fry for two minutes; the breadcrumb should be deep golden and beginning to lift away. Turn carefully with tongs, swirl again, and fry the second side for one and a half to two minutes. Lift onto a rack or kitchen paper; do not stack the schnitzels or the steam softens the crust.

Wipe the pan if any breadcrumbs are burning, top up with the rest of the butter, and repeat with the remaining cutlets. Serve with the {{lemon}} of lemon in quarters and the {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley scattered across.

items.symptom: Coating is dark and tight against the meat, not blistered
items.cause: Not enough fat in the pan; the breadcrumb fried rather than puffed.
items.fix: The fat depth needs to be around 5 mm. Top up the pan between batches if needed. Swirl the pan once after the schnitzel goes in so the fat washes over the top.

items.symptom: Coating fell off in the pan
items.cause: Breading was not pressed on, or the cutlet was not coated thoroughly through the flour stage.
items.fix: Make sure the flour layer is even before the egg goes on. The flour gives the egg something to stick to. Press the crumbs on with a flat hand after breading.

items.cause: Pounded unevenly or fried too long.
items.fix: Pound to an even 5 mm. Two minutes a side is enough for that thickness. Take the cutlet out at golden brown; do not chase darker colour.

items.cause: Drained on a flat plate, not a rack; or rested too long on kitchen paper.
items.fix: Use a wire rack on the counter so air circulates underneath. The crust stays open and crisp.

items.symptom: Butter went brown and burnt in the pan
items.cause: Heat too high, or butter went in first instead of with the oil.
items.fix: Oil first, then butter; the oil keeps the butter from scorching. If the butter burns mid-fry, wipe the pan and start the next round with fresh fat.

content.text: Veal cutlets pounded the same way give Wiener Schnitzel proper; the meat takes a little less time. Chicken breast pounded thin works on the same line for chicken schnitzel. For a Jägerschnitzel (hunter's schnitzel), serve the cutlet under a mushroom-and-onion gravy made in the same pan after frying.

Schnitzel is the everyday weeknight cutlet of central Europe, from Vienna to Warsaw, with regional names and small variations on coating and accompaniment. Traditional sides are boiled potatoes with parsley, potato salad, or a cucumber salad with sour cream. The lemon is not optional; the acid lifts the fried coating and balances the meat.
